Writing the Constructor (XbRC)

The next step to extend XbRC is to write the constructor that takes the parsed data and adds it to the class derived from XBRCRawData. In the example, this would mean writing the constructor for XBRCCubeMapData. The constructor for the new class derived from XBRCRawData takes the attributes pointer passed in and uses it to gather the data from the XML element you are parsing. Note that the attribute tags being compared are all wide-character strings. All of the error messages are also in wide-character format to help in localizing the Xbox Preview Pipeline. Here is the source for the cube map constructor:

XBRCCubeMapData::XBRCCubeMapData( ISAXAttributes *pAttributes )
{
    m_dwSize = 0;
    m_dwLevels = 0;

    m_pCubeMap = NULL;
    m_dwSizeSpecified = FALSE;

    int iAttributeCount;
    HRESULT result = pAttributes->getLength( &iAttributeCount );
    if( FAILED( result ) )
    {
               // Appropriate error handling here.
    }

    for( int iAttribute = 0; iAttribute < iAttributeCount; iAttribute++ ) 
    {
        WCHAR *pwchLocalName;
        int cchLocalName;
        result = pAttributes->getLocalName( iAttribute, &pwchLocalName, 
                                            &cchLocalName );
        if( FAILED( result ) )
        {
                    // Appropriate error handling here.
        }

        WCHAR *pwchValue;
        int cchValue;
        result = pAttributes->getValue( iAttribute, &pwchValue, &cchValue );
        if( FAILED( result ) )
        {
                    // Appropriate error handling here.
        }

        if( Match( g_strXBRC_id, pwchLocalName, cchLocalName ) )
        {
            m_strIdentifier = CXBParser::CharString( pwchValue, cchValue );
            if( m_strIdentifier.length() > 256 )
            {
                        // Appropriate warning handling here.
                m_strIdentifier.resize(256);
            }
        }
        else if( Match( g_strXBRC_Filter, pwchLocalName, cchLocalName ) )
        {
            m_strFilter  = CXBParser::CharString( pwchValue, cchValue );
        }
        else if( Match( g_strXBRC_Format, pwchLocalName, cchLocalName ) )
        {
            m_strFormat = CXBParser::CharString( pwchValue, cchValue );
        }
        else if( Match( g_strXBRC_Size, pwchLocalName, cchLocalName ) )
        {
            m_dwSize = atoi( CXBParser::CharString( pwchValue, cchValue ) );
            m_dwSizeSpecified = TRUE;
        }
        else if( Match( g_strXBRC_Levels, pwchLocalName, cchLocalName ) )
        {
            m_dwLevels = atoi( CXBParser::CharString( pwchValue, cchValue ) );
        }
        else
        {
            // Do we need to fail if we find an attribute we don't recognize?
            // Or can we just continue after informing the user?
            g_pXBParser->UnexpectedToken( pwchLocalName, cchLocalName, E_FAIL );
			g_pXBParser->m_bErrorSuppress = false;
        }
    }

    m_dwResourceType = XBRC_CUBEMAP;

    if( g_bVerbose )
    {
        WCHAR msg[1024];
        WCHAR msg2[2048];
        ZeroMemory( msg2, 2048 );
        g_LoadString( IDS_XBRC_FOUNDRESOURCE, msg, 1024 );

        _snwprintf( msg2, 2040, msg, g_strXBRC_CubeMap, 
                    m_strIdentifier.data() );

        g_pXBParser->XBRC_Message( msg2 );
    }
}

At present, it is a fatal error if you find an attribute you don't understand while parsing a particular resource type.

See Also
